stories.headline: Denver International Airport to Convert Underground Tunnel Into Pedestrian Walkways, Ending Sole Reliance on Trains
stories.summary:
Denver Mayor Michael Johnston announced Tuesday that Denver International Airport will repurpose a dormant underground baggage tunnel into pedestrian walkways, giving travelers and workers an alternative route to all concourses that does not depend on the airport's frequently troubled train system. Design work begins this year, construction is scheduled to start in 2026, and the 17-foot-wide walkways are expected to open in 2028 at an estimated cost of $300 million to $700 million.

DIA's trains have broken down 262 times over the past two years, airport records show. While most breakdowns lasted an average of four minutes, officials say the absence of a backup system is increasingly problematic as DIA expands toward a projected 120 million travelers annually by 2045.

The tunnel, built before the airport opened in 1995 and running adjacent to the train tunnel, would allow travelers to walk from the main terminal to Concourse C in 10 to 15 minutes, potentially faster if moving sidewalks are included. Airport CEO Phil Washington said artwork will be displayed in the tunnel and the experience will be designed to be welcoming rather than utilitarian.

City officials said they considered building above-ground bridges connecting concourses, but those options would cost $2 billion to $3 billion, take longer to complete, and be far more disruptive. Airlines strongly supported the tunnel approach, which Washington described as correcting the 'original sin' of building DIA without redundancy in its people-moving system.

stories.headline: Jokić Named All-NBA First Team, Murray Earns Third-Team Honor for Denver Nuggets
stories.summary:
The NBA announced that Denver Nuggets center Nikola Jokić has been named to the All-NBA First Team and guard Jamal Murray has been named to the All-NBA Third Team, as voted on by members of the media. The dual recognition underscores the Nuggets' status as one of the league's premier franchises even as the team faces continued questions about its playoff ceiling.

Jokić's selection to the first team continues a run of individual excellence that has made him one of the most decorated players in NBA history. Murray's third-team selection reflects a strong regular season from the Canadian guard, who has been one of Denver's most consistent offensive contributors.

The honors come as the Nuggets evaluate their offseason priorities heading into the summer.

stories.headline: Analysis: What Has Gone Wrong for the Avalanche in Their Stunning 3-0 Series Collapse Against Vegas
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche, who led the NHL in the standings for most of the 2025-26 season, now face elimination after falling behind the Vegas Golden Knights three games to none in the Western Conference Final. A Denver Post analysis identifies multiple compounding failures, including goaltending metrics, special teams inefficiency, and key injuries, as factors in the collapse.

Goaltender Scott Wedgewood, a feel-good story throughout the season, has been outplayed by Vegas counterpart Carter Hart in all three games. Hart's goals saved above expected for the series stands at plus-3.0, while Wedgewood sits at minus-1.66, a gap that has proven decisive in a tightly contested series where Colorado has frequently outchanced Vegas but failed to convert.

Only four teams in NHL history have overcome a 3-0 series deficit, two of them in the past 50 years. Avalanche coach Jared Bednar acknowledged the situation is surprising given the team's ability to produce clutch plays throughout the season, but said there is still a path forward if the team can string together the same late-game execution that defined their regular season.

stories.headline: Xcel Energy Points to Google Data Center Deal as Template for Managing Colorado's Large-Load Power Surge
stories.summary:
Xcel Energy is using a deal it struck with Google for a large data center as a model for how the utility intends to handle a surge in high-capacity electricity customers in Colorado while protecting existing ratepayers from cost increases. The approach involves negotiating individualized agreements that shift infrastructure costs to large commercial customers rather than spreading them across the general rate base.

Colorado is experiencing growing demand from data centers and other large industrial users attracted by the state's tech economy and available land. Xcel's CEO indicated the Google agreement demonstrates that it is possible to accommodate significant new load without triggering broad rate increases for residential and small business customers.

The strategy is being watched closely by state regulators and consumer advocates as Colorado weighs how to balance economic development with utility affordability.

stories.headline: Three Robbery Suspects Hospitalized After Pursuit Ends in Crash on I-70 in Commerce City
stories.summary:
Three robbery suspects were hospitalized with serious injuries Monday after the stolen vehicle they were in crashed into a retaining wall during a police pursuit on Interstate 70 in Commerce City. The suspects are believed to have robbed multiple people at gunpoint and at knifepoint outside a home in the 6100 block of Oneida Street overnight.

Commerce City Police said the victims were sitting in a car parked at a home when the suspects robbed them. After the victims called police, the suspects returned to the scene in two separate stolen vehicles. Officers attempted to stop both cars, prompting a pursuit that continued onto I-70 before one vehicle exited at Pecos Street and crashed.

Two of the three occupants of the crashed vehicle have been identified: one is 16 years old and one is 18. The third has not been identified. Police are still searching for four additional suspects who fled in the second stolen vehicle, which was not involved in the crash.

stories.headline: Man Arrested After Driving Through Secured Gate at Denver International Airport, Resisting Officers
stories.summary:
A 34-year-old man was arrested early Monday after Denver Police said he drove a white Lexus through a secured employee gate at Denver International Airport and later resisted officers attempting to take him into custody. Christopher Leonard Alonzo first drew attention around 2:13 a.m. when officers were dispatched to a disturbance near 8500 Peña Boulevard.

According to a probable cause statement, Alonzo attempted to back his vehicle toward a marked patrol car before driving forward into an airport operations building loading dock gate, causing significant damage and entering a secured employee parking lot. Officers later found Alonzo hiding behind an electrical box inside the secured area.

Alonzo resisted arrest by kicking at officers and tensing his arms as they attempted to handcuff him. He was taken into custody and is being held on multiple charges. The incident remains under investigation by Denver Police.

stories.headline: Fatal Two-Vehicle Crash Near Denver International Airport Kills One, Injures Four
stories.summary:
A head-on collision on 120th Avenue near North Powhaton Road, just north of Denver International Airport, left one person dead and four others injured Sunday afternoon, Colorado State Patrol said. The crash occurred just before 3 p.m. when a westbound Chevrolet Silverado crossed into the eastbound lane.

The 48-year-old male driver of the Silverado, a Denver resident, was pronounced dead at the scene. His 28-year-old passenger, also from Denver, was transported to a hospital with life-threatening injuries. The driver of the Ford F-150, a 62-year-old man from Bennett, sustained serious injuries, and two of his passengers were also hospitalized.

The road was closed for more than three hours before reopening around 6:30 p.m. Investigators have not yet identified a cause, and the Adams County Coroner's Office will conduct an autopsy and notify next of kin before releasing the identity of the deceased driver. Anyone with information is asked to contact Colorado State Patrol at 303-239-4501.

stories.headline: Aurora Apartment Fire Displaces 50 Residents, Injures Two
stories.summary:
A fire tore through an Aurora apartment building early Tuesday, displacing approximately 50 residents and injuring two people. Specific details about the cause of the fire and the extent of the injuries were not immediately available.

The incident adds to a pattern of residential fires in the Denver metro area that have strained emergency housing resources. Displaced residents were being assisted in finding temporary accommodations following the blaze.

stories.headline: Castle Rock Man Dies on Manitou Incline During Cub Scout Outing With 9-Year-Old Son
stories.summary:
Nickolay Jogolev, a 48-year-old Castle Rock resident and father of seven children, died Saturday after experiencing breathing problems while climbing the Manitou Incline with his son's Cub Scout pack. El Paso County Sheriff's deputies cleared the trail just before 10 a.m. so first responders could reach him, but Jogolev died later at a hospital. The cause of death is pending autopsy results.

Jogolev had completed the same hike with the Cub Scouts the previous year and had participated in all preparatory training hikes. His wife, Coral Jogolev, described him as healthy — he never smoked or drank — and said his death was entirely unexpected. His 9-year-old son, who was with him on the trail, is described as taking the loss especially hard.

Jogolev immigrated to the United States from Russia in his early 20s and worked in IT for CommonSpirit Health. He was the family's sole financial provider, and his wife is now facing significant financial hardship. The Manitou Incline, located at the base of Pikes Peak near Colorado Springs, gains 2,000 feet of elevation over less than a mile and has seen previous fatalities.

stories.headline: Colorado Pine Beetle Infestation Expanded 150% in 2025, State Forester Report Warns of Exponential Spread
stories.summary:
A new Colorado State Forest Service report shows that pine beetles chewed through nearly 150% more Front Range ponderosa pine forest in 2025 than in 2024, and State Forester Matt McCombs warned the destruction will soon become exponential. The report projects widespread loss of the ponderosa-dominated forests along the Front Range over the next two decades, compounding wildfire risk as dead timber accumulates.

The beetle's advance through ponderosa follows its earlier devastation of Colorado's lodgepole forests in the 2000s and 2010s, a die-off that contributed to the destructive 2020 East Troublesome and Cameron Peak wildfires. McCombs said the state's resources are not sufficient to save a large portion of the affected forests, though some landmark stands and individual property trees can be protected through pesticide injections.

Separately, the report confirms that the emerald ash borer has spread to six new Colorado cities — Aurora, Berthoud, Denver, Edgewater, Golden, and Wheat Ridge — and is expected to reach Castle Rock and Colorado Springs within a few years. Urban foresters warn that ash trees make up as much as 15% of the tree canopy in many affected cities, and the borer is now present in at least 20 Colorado communities since its discovery in Boulder in 2013.

stories.headline: Former Marines from Greeley Area Honor Veterans with Burial Tributes at Fort Logan National Cemetery
stories.summary:
A veterans service organization called the Union Colony Marines Detachment 1093, based in the Greeley area, regularly travels to Fort Logan National Cemetery in Denver to render full military honors at burial services for veterans of all service branches. The honor guard, composed mostly of men in their 70s and 80s, participates in as many as a dozen services per day at Fort Logan and has averaged more than 140 services annually in recent years.

The group performs three-round rifle salutes and the playing of taps at each service, presenting a commemorative medallion and spent cartridges to the veteran's family. Members say they volunteer purely out of respect, regardless of what branch the deceased served in.

The feature, published ahead of Memorial Day, profiles the group's decades of service and the personal meaning members attach to their work, including one longtime member who estimated he attended more than 600 burial services over seven years. Fort Logan National Cemetery is the final resting place of more than 160,000 veterans and eligible family members.

stories.headline: Drought and Mild Winter Point to More Intense Miller Moth Season Along Colorado's Front Range
stories.summary:
Colorado's ongoing drought combined with a mild winter are expected to produce a more intense miller moth migration along the Front Range this year, according to entomologists and Colorado State University extension experts. Fewer army cutworm larvae died over the winter due to warmer temperatures, while drought has reduced wildflower availability, pushing more moths toward irrigated residential areas in search of nectar.

Karim Gharbi, CSU's Denver County extension agent, said he anticipates more moths overall and higher concentrations near homes with irrigated flowering plants. Butterfly Pavilion lepidopterist Shiran Hershcovich cautioned that predicting exact moth volume is difficult and encouraged residents to approach the annual migration with curiosity rather than alarm.

Miller moths, which are harmless to humans, serve as pollinators and are an important food source for birds and even bears along the Front Range. Experts advise residents to turn off outdoor lights at night to help moths stay on their migration path toward mountain meadows, and to seal gaps in homes to prevent moths from becoming trapped indoors.

joke.joke: Getting in shape is easy. It's getting out of round that's the problem.

topStory.headline: U.S. Strikes Iranian Missile Sites and Naval Vessels Near Strait of Hormuz
topStory.summary:
The United States launched new military strikes against targets in southern Iran on Monday, hitting Iranian missile sites and boats that U.S. forces said were attempting to lay mines. U.S. Central Command described the action as self-defense, with spokesperson Capt. Tim Hawkins stating the strikes were conducted "to protect our troops from threats posed by Iranian forces" while maintaining what he called restraint under an ongoing ceasefire.

The strikes targeted an area near Bandar Abbas, a strategic southern port city that houses an Iranian naval base on the Strait of Hormuz. Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps responded Tuesday by claiming it had shot down a U.S. drone and fired on a fighter jet and a second drone that it said entered Iranian airspace, though it did not specify the timing of those incidents. The IRGC also asserted that Iran held the "legitimate and definite" right to retaliate against what it characterized as ceasefire violations by the United States.

Despite the exchange of fire, Secretary of State Marco Rubio, speaking to reporters during an official visit to India, said a diplomatic agreement between Washington and Tehran remained possible. He pointed to ongoing talks Tuesday between Iran's foreign minister and Qatar's prime minister, describing the negotiations as focused on specific language in an initial framework document. Rubio stressed that keeping the Strait of Hormuz open was non-negotiable, calling Iranian mining activity "unlawful, illegal, and unsustainable for the world."

Iran's Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei, who has not appeared publicly since succeeding his late father earlier this year, issued a statement Tuesday during the annual Hajj pilgrimage declaring that the Middle East "will no longer serve as shields for U.S. bases." His remarks signaled continued defiance from Tehran's leadership even as diplomatic channels remain open. Earlier in May, a separate naval clash between U.S. destroyers and Iranian forces in the Strait of Hormuz had prompted President Trump to insist a ceasefire remained in effect.

The latest strikes add new uncertainty to fragile negotiations over Iran's nuclear program and regional military posture. Trump said over the weekend that a deal appeared close, but subsequently instructed negotiators not to rush. The repeated military incidents in and around the Strait of Hormuz — a chokepoint for roughly a fifth of global oil supplies — underscore the high stakes should diplomacy fail to produce an agreement in the coming days.
